Name: informationtheoreticmodelcompressionbounds
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Model Compression: Achieving Optimal Distortion-Rate Trade-offs
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive tighter theoretical bounds that predict the optimal trade-off between compression rate and task performance. This will provide a principled way to evaluate and improve existing compression methods.
Related Work: Existing work has explored the rate-distortion framework for model compression, showing that pruning and other compression techniques can be understood in terms of reducing generalization error while increasing empirical risk (Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2019). However, these studies often focus on specific algorithms and empirical evaluations. Our proposal aims to derive more general and tighter theoretical bounds that can be applied to a wider range of compression techniques and models.
Abstract: We propose a novel information-theoretic approach to model compression that derives tighter theoretical bounds on the rate-distortion trade-off. By treating model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we aim to quantify the minimum achievable distortion (task performance degradation) for a given compression rate. Our approach leverages mutual information and entropy estimators to measure the task-relevant information preserved during compression. We will derive achievable and converse bounds, and propose a method to optimize the compression process to stay close to these bounds. Our work will provide a principled framework for evaluating and improving existing compression techniques, ensuring they are as close as possible to the theoretical limits.
Experiments: Derive achievable and converse bounds for the rate-distortion trade-off in model compression using mutual information and entropy estimators.
Implement a compression algorithm based on the derived bounds and compare its performance with existing methods on popular datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
Evaluate the compression algorithm using distortion-rate curves and compare the achieved trade-offs with the theoretical bounds.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different distortion metrics and compression techniques on the rate-distortion trade-off.
Analyze the generalization performance of compressed models and compare it with the theoretical predictions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds may be difficult to compute for complex models, and approximations may be necessary.
The choice of distortion metric can significantly affect the results, and finding a universally applicable metric is challenging.
The proposed method may require more computational resources for deriving and optimizing the bounds compared to simpler empirical techniques.
The practical implementation of the theoretical bounds may not always achieve the predicted performance due to real-world constraints and approximations.
